data.title: Intuitive Machines: Success After Lunar Failure
data.description:
Intuitive Machines (NASDAQ:LUNR) surprised Wall Street with positive free cash flow during in Q1 2025. The stock has been rewarded with a 22.4% gain, but still trades well below its highs from January. In March, I reiterated my strong buy rating for LUNR stock and that has worked out rather well with a 24% gain so far.

As I discuss in this report, the company has a lot of opportunities but also a lot of trust to recover after two moon missions that did not go smoothly, which is a soft way to put it.

LUNR Fixing The Moon Mission

Intuitive Machines stock lost altitude after its second moon landing mission, IM-2, ended up landing on its side in March, ending the mission early. After the first mission, which saw the lander tipping over, expectations were high for success on the second mission, but it ended in an utter disappointment. The stock price plunged on the news in a market environment that already signalled lower risk appetite.

The company has addressed three points that required improvement to assure a precise and soft landing. The first is laser altimeter interference, the second being lighting conditions, and the third is crater recognition. For the third mission, the company has added additional altimeters to its sensor package, added a lighting independent sensor for sensing surface velocity and the crater database has been enhanced.

So, there are a lot of useful changes that were made to increase the chances of success for the third mission, scheduled for later this year. The reality remains that the company is booking progress, but the IM-2 was underwhelming.

During the Q1 2025 earnings call, CEO Steve Altemus said he believes IM-3 will be a success:

Moving forward, we will succeed, land softly, land upright, land ready to operate. We've identified the issues and are making the necessary changes we believe will get us there on IM-3.

While I do believe the sensor suite has been significantly enhanced to increase chances of mission success, I do not put a lot of credence to the CEO’s statement on mission success. The reason for that is that management’s response in the wake of the disappointing IM-2 was not strong.

Intuitive Machines Has Major Opportunities Ahead

Intuitive Machines believes that more work and contracts being outsourced to the private sector creates opportunities for the likes of Intuitive Machines. I would not disagree with that assessment, but do note that when it comes to the track record for the moon missions IM does not have an outstanding one and the reality is that outsourcing in some cases, just like with the commercial lunar payload services happens at a fixed-price basis. We do not know whether the sixth contract that is expected to be awarded later this year has the same contract set up. What we do know is that fixed-price contracts transfer all risks to the contractor.

That does not mean that there are no opportunities ahead. The company has two CLPS awards that it competes for this year that could be meaningful if won. The first one is CS6, the sixth payload delivery mission, and the second one is from the Science Mission Directorate. Both are part of the Commercial Lunar Payload Services program.

Towards the end of the year the LTV or Lunar Terrain Vehicle contract will be awarded if the program is set to continue and additional task orders for NSNS to set up a relay network are also to be expected with Task Order 2 valued $18 million being awarded in April. The company is also working on a nuclear-powered satellite, which is of national security interested given that it would mean that solar panels are no longer required, making the satellites less observable. The company is also working adding Earth re-entry capability, which could come in handy for some space objectives including microgravity research where the experiment has to be returned to Earth after experiments have been conducted.

Another opportunity is the OSAM part of OMES III. Intuitive Machines won the Omnibus Multidisciplinary Engineering Services III, which provided a significant and consistent revenue stream, but NASA ended up cancelling the OSAM part of the contract. OSAM stands for On-orbit Servicing, Assembly and Manufacturing. Intuitive Machines is now looking at whether it can apply OSAM to defense customers to revive that revenue stream.

Fourth Quarter Loss Widens Despite Revenue Growth

While Intuitive Machines is in growth mode, its revenues are not. First quarter revenues decreased $10.7 million. The decision from NASA to cancel the OSAM project within the OMES III contract drove a $20.1 million decrease in revenues for that contract while the CLPS mission contracts revenues declined $1.5 million partially offset by higher lunar terrain vehicle revenues of $6.9 million and NSN in the amount of $3 million while other engineering services increased $1 million.

The CLPS decline is driven by the fact that the company could recognize $12.3 million in restricted revenue for IM-1 in the same period last year, but the IM-2 restricted revenues are slipping into Q2 with half of the $14.4 million expected in Q2. The decrease in IM-1 revenues was partially offset by revenues for IM-2 through IM-4.

Cost of revenues decreased by $3.4 million driven by the lower revenue base, resulting in a $10.1 million operating loss. Pre-tax income rose from a $118 million loss to a $1 million profit driven by lower loss on securities and a positive impact of the fair value of the warrants that the company redeemed, partially offset by higher earn out liabilities.

Driven by timing of receipts, the company saw a $13.3 million free cash flow compared to $8 million a year ago, ending the quarter with $373.3 million in cash driven by proceeds of $176.6 million from warrants exercised.

The positive is that the company no longer has significant pressure to raise capital as it also secured a $40 million liquidity line and all warrants are exercised. Which should bode well for investors as the company eyes positive run rate EBITDA by the end of the year and positive EBITDA for 2026.

What Is Intuitive Machines Stock Worth?

For Intuitive Machines, I am using a P/S valuation method using revenue estimates for the years ahead. Against its own median price-to-sales ratio, the stock is not worth much and also, when we look at the industry, LUNR stock appears overvalued. However, I do believe that Intuitive Machines is the kind of company that has significant potential and should be valued in line with Rocket Lab, which I consider the gold standard in space opportunities.

By doing so and applying the minimum P/S of 6.1x for RKLB to LUNR, we get price targets of $14.70 for 2025 and $20 for 2026. So, I am maintaining my strong buy rating for now.

Conclusion: Intuitive Machines Needs To Deliver

The first quarter results showed a decline in revenues driven by the IM-2 constrained revenues slipping into Q2 and lower OMES III contract revenues. That is disappointing. However, we do see higher LTV and NSN revenues flowing into the system and, overall, Intuitive Machines has the opportunity to decouple its revenue growth from payload delivery services where it has yet to meet expectations. We also keep in mind any pivot away from lunar exploration in favor of targeting visits to Mars by the current administration, but do note that lunar exploration is an ideal springboard to expand to exploration of Mars. A positive during the quarter was the positive free cash flow, but this was largely driven by the timing of receipts.

While we keep in mind the risk of the IM-3 mission, I do believe that the company remains a strong buy as it has key contract awards ahead and task orders to delivery on, with new task orders in the pipeline.

data.title: SPY: Expected Economic Aftershocks Make Me A Bear Right Now
data.description:
U.S. stock markets, as commonly represented by the SPDR S&P 500 Trust (NYSEARCA:SPY), have rallied substantially from the trade policy hurricane that hit in early April. While there was incredible uncertainty in capital markets a month ago, and even famed investor Steve Eisman argued that investors should adopt a "Don't Be A Hero" mentality, equity indices have climbed back to levels even higher than when the Trump Administration's “Liberation Day” tariff announcements sent the market reeling.

I'm not terribly surprised by the recovery, for a plurality of reasons:

  • No matter his recent insistence to the contrary, I never doubted the importance that President Trump places on stock market performance. He promoted strong stock market performance during his first term as U.S. President, for example. Eventually, his administration was going to be swayed by market stress.
  • Punitive tariffs on important trading partners were naturally going to impact the everyday lives of Americans eventually, and major retailers' warning of “empty shelves” in the near term hurried the need for a tariff resolution.
  • Retail investors have had 1.5 decades of proof (in their minds) that market pullbacks are always worth buying, "no questions asked". I didn't expect them to abandon that approach without experiencing significant personal economic pain (which hasn't hit—April's strong jobs report makes that clear).

Monday's (April 12) heroics following the announcement of a thawing of U.S.-China trade tensions (including a 90-day pause of reciprocal tariffs) have brought the S&P 500 within earshot of YTD gains. Three-quarters of the S&P's 20% decline from February 19th's all-time high has now been recovered.

The remainder of this article examines and predicts where U.S. stock markets may go from here.

Revisiting Beginning-Of-Year Expectations

Despite having been bearish on U.S. stock markets in recent years, and the fact that I've been actively hedging away equity upside exposure for income, in late-December 2024, I presented an upside outlook for the S&P 500 for this year. In fact, I was predicting about a 10%-12% upside on top of the ~57.5% total return that the SPY has offered investors for the two-year period between January 1, 2023, and January 1, 2025.

In "2025 Markets To Be Driven By Liquidity, Lobbying And Collectibility - Most Likely", I predicted a year-end S&P 500 Index value of 6,440.

S&P 500 Earnings Estimates

Coming into 2025, Wall Street Analysts were expecting S&P 500 earnings to clock in at $250.66 for the year (see above-linked article for details). At the time, those forecasts represented nearly a full 20% increase in earnings as compared to 2024.

Here is how I viewed the catalysts as the calendar turned:

Tailwinds for 2025 earnings include:

  • AI efficiency gains, as various industries apply artificial intelligence solutions;
  • Lower interest rates (than last year);
  • Lower corporate tax rates (potentially).

Headwinds for 2025 earnings include:

  • Strong USD (DXY) economics, as U.S. products become more expensive, and as transnationals' international earnings translate to less in USD terms;
  • Supply chain issues due to a possible implementation of tariffs;
  • Strain on U.S. consumers from higher prices due to tariffs;
  • The cost side of implementing AI solutions (it's a big investment);
  • Already record-high corporate margins.

-Source: my December 20, 2024 article.

Here's an interesting question for readers to ponder: Given all that has transpired during the first ~4 months of 2025, how do you think S&P 500 estimates have changed? Per Ycharts, current expectations stand at $238.44, which represents about a -4.9% decline.

On this basis, the index was recently trading at a 24.1x multiple. That remains at the all-time highest levels in history, excluding recessionary periods.

While U.S. GDP did fall -0.3% for Q1 2025, the economy isn't (yet) formally in recession. Nonetheless, some investors may reasonably argue that 2025 earnings will be temporarily depressed and that 2026 will be a period of greater corporate normalcy. That seems to play out in the forward earnings estimates of $274.19 for the S&P 500 next year. If current expectations are realized, earnings will grow +13.5% this year, and +15% in 2026. Next year's estimates would equate to a forward P/E of just under 21x at recent S&P 500 levels.

Why Now Might Be A Good Time To Worry

When I presented my 2025 S&P forecast at the end of last year, I was readily aware of the possibility that both higher consumer prices and supply chain strains from tariffs could pose meaningful challenges to Corporate America.

Attempting to put myself back into my own shoes from late-December 2024, I think current (effective) U.S. import tariffs on foreign goods would be within my band of expectations. What I would never have guessed, however, was the path that has taken us to this point, including 100%+ tariffs on Chinese goods entering the United States for about a month. I also wouldn't have expected the smorgasbord of temporarily high reciprocal tariffs on a variety of other countries for a period of time.

Officially (at least based on what has been made public), those ex-China reciprocal tariffs are on a 90-day pause ending in early July, and the China reciprocal tariffs (beyond 30%, which is still significant) are on pause for 90 days effective this week.

Generally speaking, I do not believe that these punitive reciprocal tariffs will be reactivated after those 90-day pauses. I think the capital markets drama of the past ~6 weeks, and an upcoming economic fallout, to make the Trump Administration a bit more gun-shy. With that said....

  • Inevitable and highly public declarations that the Trump Administration has lost the trade war will likely result in the U.S. President making additional threats against foreign countries. I strongly expect this and that it will cause further economic instability across various lands, including the United States.
  • I think some investors believe that the revocation of high reciprocal tariffs suddenly puts us in the clear. I don't view it that way at all. A highly integrated global economy has experienced supply-chain dislocations whose effects have now hit the main street economy, in my opinion. I expect the tariff ordeal to resemble an earthquake with aftershocks, and those aftershocks could have meaningful consequences on confidence and investment.

Why Unhedged Foreign ETFs like VFV:CA Look Particularly Vulnerable

If I'm turning bearish on the S&P 500 now, I'm especially bearish on foreign tracking ETFs that benefit from a strong U.S. Dollar. We've already seen strong evidence of capital flows moving to places other than the United States. Even if the tariff drama is on the backburner, I think global confidence in the world's most powerful country has been injured for years to come.

Furthermore, while the Trump Administration's objective was to repatriate capital investment dollars back to the United States, businesses may now be reluctant to commit to investing in the country due to policy unpredictability.

Tracking ETFs like Vanguard's VFV:CA have outperformed the S&P 500 in recent years, and the main reason for that has been the depreciating Canadian Dollar.

Meanwhile, currency-hedged ETFs like Vanguard's U.S. Total Market Index ETF (VUS:CA) have underperformed in recent years.

I'm expecting the switch to flip, and underpinning an appreciating Canadian Dollar (which is my expectation) may be suddenly patriotic Canadians' decisions to limit their spending south of the border (on either products or travel). Canadian businesses may opt out of renewing supply contracts with American exporters when they come due, and I don't expect lost tourism dollars to find their way States-bound again anytime soon.

Conclusion And Recommendation

After clawing back all of their losses since “Liberty Day”, major U.S. stock indices seem at risk here, especially if aftershocks from the trade wars hit the economy in the next few months.

Meanwhile, the U.S. Dollar looks rich as investment capital seems destined to continue to look for a new home away from the unpredictability of the United States.

I'm converting to a bearish view on broad U.S. markets, and am especially pessimistic about foreign-based ETFs that track the S&P 500 without currency hedges.

data.title: A Pair Trade Opportunity By Bank of America's Preferred Stocks
data.description:
Co-authored by Relative Value.

Overview

The US tariff announcement in early April 2025 stirred almost all market sectors and product types. Currently, the general market, represented by the S&P 500 index, has recovered from the high-volume selling that the tariff uncertainty caused and is priced above its values from before the announcement. This is not the case with the fixed-income market, especially the long-term securities. The selling pressure in the longest-term treasuries caused equalizing in the call options' values within a group of similar long-duration fixed-income products with the same issuer, effectively making them trade with very close yields. The deviation from the long-term yield spread between the same types of securities gives us a profit opportunity when these spreads mean-revert. With this short article, we'll present such a deviation from Bank of America Corporation's (NYSE:BAC) listed perpetual preferred stocks - the 6.00% Depositary Shares Non-Cumulative Preferred Stock Series GG, Bank of America Corporation 6 NCUM PFD SR GG (NYSE:BAC.PR.B) and the 4.125% Depositary Shares Non-Cumulative Preferred Stock Series PP, Bank of America Corporation 4.125% DP PFD PP (NYSE:BAC.PR.P) and take a look at the potential expected profit.

Bank of America Overview

Bank of America, a Delaware corporation, headquartered in Charlotte, North Carolina, is a leading financial holding company on a global scale and the second-largest bank by asset size in the US. The company provides a wide range of financial services in the US and international markets through its business segments: Consumer Banking, Global Wealth and Investment Management, Global Banking and Global Markets, and All Other. Bank of America is considered systemically important for the US financial system, and as such, it is included in the "Too Big to Fail" list. The price chart below shows how the general public has evaluated the common stock of the company for the past ten years:

If we compare the total return performance of Bank of America to that of the S&P 500 and SPDR® S&P Bank ETF (KBE) for the same period, we get the following chart:

Bank of America's total performance over the past ten years seems to have been moving with the market's general trend, outperforming on the upswing and underperforming on the downswing. As the general trend was up, it should come as no surprise that BAC's total return is better than the S&P 500's. The performance of the company compared to the average US bank, represented by the KBE ETF, is more impressive - Bank of America has outperformed twice the sector in the total return metric.

Regarding the future valuation of Bank of America, the SA Analysts are giving it the neutral "Hold" rating, the Quant Rating gives a bullish "Buy" score, while according to the Wall Street analysts, the company is a real bargain at this valuation and give it the "Strong Buy" rating.

If we look at the Quant Rating Factor Grades of the company and compare them to the valuations it received six months ago, we see deterioration in the Valuation and Momentum markings, and improvement in Growth and Earnings revisions. The strongest mark the company gets for its Profitability performance, as this was the worst score six months ago. The improvement is due mainly to the better TTM cash from operations of the company, compared to six months ago. The $4.55 billion of cash from operations of Bank of America is way above the average $155 million for the sector, but still far below the average value of $19.4 billion the company has managed to accomplish in the past five years.

Taking the comparison from above a step further, we can put the total return performance of Bank of America against other big diversified banks:

Bank of America's performance is second only to JPMorgan Chase & Co.'s (JPM) over the ten-year period. The two biggest US banks had almost identical total returns until the end of 2022, but since then, JPM has outperformed significantly and currently has almost twice the total return BAC has.

Company Credibility

Fitch Ratings - New York - 12 Jun 2024: Fitch Ratings has affirmed Bank of America Corporation's (BAC) Long- and Short-Term Issuer Default Rating (IDRs) at 'AA-' and 'F1+', respectively. Additionally, Fitch has affirmed Bank of America, N.A.'s (BANA) Long- and Short-Term IDRs at 'AA' and 'F1+', respectively. The Rating Outlook on the Long-Term IDRs is Stable. A full list of rating actions is below.

Strong Intrinsic Profile: BAC's intrinsic creditworthiness, as expressed by its Viability Rating (VR) of 'aa-', drives its IDRs. The VR reflects BAC's leading franchise in many of its core businesses, its diversified business mix and consistent strategy, which Fitch believes will continue to underpin a more stable through-the-cycle financial performance relative to peers. BAC's VR is one notch above the implied VR of 'a+' due to the company's business profile, which Fitch assesses at 'aa-' and considers a high influence factor in its ratings.

This is the beginning of the recent rating report with which Fitch affirms the 'AA-' long-term issuer default rating with a stable outlook for Bank of America. The rating history for the past couple of years of the company is depicted below. Bank of America's last credit upgrade was four years ago in June 2021, and since then, according to Fitch Ratings, there have been no substantial changes in the credit quality of the corporation.

Capital Structure

Bank of America recently issued an earnings report for the First Quarter of 2025, so we can see the company's actual capital structure. The capital structure for the previous four quarters is also provided for comparison purposes, so anyone interested can see how it evolved historically in the short term.

As of Q1'25, Bank of America had a total debt of $773.8 billion, ranking senior to the outstanding preferred stock equity of approximately $20.5 billion. At the lowest position in the company's capital structure is the $275.1 billion of common equity. Calculated this way, Debt-to-Equity for Bank of America amounts to 2.81 - a higher value than the average of 1.23 for the diversified banks sector in the US as of May 2025.

Dividends

Bank of America has an 11-year history of increasing common stock dividends and, even more impressive, 30 years of uninterrupted dividend payments. This is what the dividend history of the company looks like:

The twelve-month trailing dividend distributed to BAC's shareholders amounts to $1.02 in the form of quarterly payments. With the current price of the stock at $43.36, that corresponds to a 2.35% dividend yield. This is how the dividend yield looks for the past 10 years:

And the forward estimations for the dividend and dividend yield:

Bank of America has approximately 7.8 billion common shares outstanding and a $1.04 forward estimated annual payment per share. We can calculate around $8.1 billion forward annualized distribution for the common stockholders. The total $1.5 billion annual dividend for the preferred shares of the company represents approximately 18.5% of that sum. The cushion that the common shares distributions present is large enough for the preferred shareholders to feel considerably confident that they will receive their payments in the foreseeable future.

Bank of America Bonds

Next, we want to take a look at the debt of Bank of America. The corporation currently has close to one hundred debt issues trading on the bond market.

The long-term senior debt of Bank of America is rated 'A1' by Moody's and is priced with a yield to worst of approximately 5.8%, or 90 basis points above the 20-year treasuries.

As we can see from the average risk premium for the single-A rated US corporate debt currently, Bank of America's long-term debt is priced quite in line with its peers with similar credit quality. Nothing currently indicates that the bank's creditors have any reason to demand a higher yield from the company's debt.

BAC preferred stocks pair trade

As we noted in our recent article, pair trades among the same types of securities of a common issuer are among the safest bets credit-wise anyone can make on the exchange. Due to the different valuations of the embedded call options, the securities are trading with a long-term average yield spread. From time to time, however, the up shifts in the respective treasury benchmark cause the difference in the call options evaluations to narrow and even disappear. From that point onward, on a continuous up shift in the benchmark yield, the securities tend to move together on the downside, keeping almost identical yields. On a downshift in the benchmark yield, the call options tend to return to their long-term values, so our expectancy is that the long leg in our pair trade should appreciate more in value than the short one.

Bank of America currently has ten listed outstanding preferred stocks - all of them but BAC-E are perpetuities with fixed rates of payment. In the chart below are listed the relevant metrics for the securities. The fixed-rate perpetuities with a PAR value of $25 are priced within a very tight current yield spread.

Due to the differences in their call option prices under normal market conditions, BAC-B and BAC-K usually trade with higher current yields than the rest of the fixed-rate $25 PAV value preferreds. As explained above, this valuation changes in times of intense selling in fixed-income products, when the call options of the preferred stocks in the group grow closer in value, and the CY spread disappears. The chart below shows the historical current yields of BAC-B in blue and BAC-P in red, since the IPO of BAC-P. May 2022, October 2022, and October 2023 are examples of such occasions when the call option price difference in the two securities disappears, and their current yield metrics become equal.

These were the times when the current yield spread of the two preferred stocks disappears, and they start moving together on the downside. Sooner or later, they mean-revert with BAC-P having more potential on the upside.

The reason for this, as stated above, is that when the selling pressure is over, the reason for the call options prices to be equal is gone with it, and the securities CY spread mean reverts, returning to its long-term values. This gives the trader who takes a long position in BAC-P and short in BAC-B the opportunity to try to exploit the deviation and subsequent normalization.

The chart above shows the average CY spread between BAC-B and BAC-P of 61 basis points in favor of BAC-B's metric. Currently, BAC-P's current yield is 13 basis points higher than BAC-B's, indicating a potential of 74 basis points for the pair trade. That corresponds to approximately $2.20 or over 13% of potential per share for the (BAC-P long - BAC-B short) pair trade. We have the more expensive security in the short leg, so the pair trade is effectively credit-risk free.

One of the biggest problems with the pair trades is the carry cost, so it has to be considered in all cases. Currently, BAC-B has a borrowing fee of approximately 3%, or a $0.06 cost per month of holding the short position. This is not a whole lot, looking at a $2.20 potential reward, and that the previous times the current yields of the two securities mean-reverted within a couple of months. However, as we should well know, in this line of business, there are no guarantees - neither that the borrowing fee will stay this low, nor that the pair trade's potential will be closed in the foreseeable future.

Conclusion

This is a short article, with which we want to turn your attention to the pair trade opportunity that BAC-P and BAC-B are currently offering, similar to a couple of times in the past few years. We looked into the reasons why on the upside, BAC-P has more potential than BAC-B, and on the downside, both securities should move together. Both stocks are among the most liquid preferreds on the exchange, so getting in and out of the position should not be a problem. The 13% credit risk-free potential is appealing for risk-averse investors. However, discretion is required, as the carry costs could liquidate the trade's potential.

data.title: IXJ: Healthcare Sector Dashboard For May
data.description:
This monthly article reports metrics in the healthcare sector, aiming at a top-down analysis based on value, quality, and momentum. It may also help analyze sector ETFs such as the Health Care Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLV) and iShares Global Healthcare ETF (NYSEARCA:IXJ), whose holdings have been used to calculate these metrics.

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italic describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short on time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios for each industry: Earnings Yield (EY), Sales Yield (SY), Free Cash Flow Yield (FY), Return on Equity (ROE), and Gross Margin (GM). The reference universe includes large companies in the U.S. stock market. The five base metrics are calculated on the trailing 12 months. For all of them, higher is better. EY, SY, and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales, and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable or unavailable when the “something” is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also look at two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians to averages because a median splits a set into a good half and a bad half. A capital-weighted average is skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. My metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value and Quality Scores

I calculate historical baselines for all metrics. They are noted respectively as EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, GMh, and they are calculated as the averages over a look-back period of 11 years. For example, the value of EYh for healthcare providers in the table below is the 11-year average of the median Earnings Yield in this industry.

The Value Score (VS) is defined as the average difference in % between the three valuation ratios (EY, SY, FY) and their baselines (EYh, SYh, FYh). The same way, the Quality Score (QS) is the average difference between the two quality ratios (ROE, GM) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

The scores are in percentage points. VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the three valuation metrics are of equal importance.

Current data

The next table shows the metrics and scores as of writing. Columns stand for all the data defined above.

Value and Quality chart

The next chart plots the Value and Quality Scores by industry (higher is better).

Evolution since last month

The value score has improved for healthcare providers and pharmaceuticals/biotechnology. The quality score has also greatly improved for the latter.

Momentum

The next chart plots median returns by subsector.

Interpretation

Based on my latest S&P 500 dashboard, the healthcare sector as a whole is slightly undervalued relative to 11-year averages. The healthcare providers industry is undervalued by about 15%. The pharmaceuticals/biotechnology subsector is close to its historical baseline in valuation and shows a good quality score. Healthcare equipment is moderately overvalued, with a quality score above the baseline.

Fast facts on IXJ

iShares Global Healthcare ETF (IXJ) was launched on 11/13/2001 and tracks the S&P Global 1200 Health Care Index. IXJ invests in worldwide healthcare companies, weighted based on float-adjusted market capitalization. It has 115 holdings and an expense ratio of 0.41%, while the U.S. sector benchmark XLV charges only 0.08%. U.S. companies represent 70.9% of asset value, Switzerland weighs 8.5%, and other countries are below 5%.

The portfolio is quite concentrated. Indeed, the top 10 holdings, listed in the next table, represent 41.5% of asset value, and the four heaviest positions are between 4% and 9%. Risks related to other individual companies are low.

data.title: Capitol Federal Financial Is Still Cheap Where It Matters Most
data.description:
Way back in September of 2023, one bank that I decided to analyze was Capitol Federal Financial, Inc. (NASDAQ:CFFN). At the time, the firm had experienced significant downside because of the banking crisis that occurred earlier in the year. Despite many of the companies in the industry recovering, it had lagged, with shares still down 36% since February. At the end of the day, I believed that the stock was cheap enough and the company was of a high enough quality to justify a bullish assessment. This led me to rate it a "Buy."

Unfortunately, the picture has not turned out quite as well as I would have hoped for. Even though overall financial performance for the institution continues to improve, low asset quality and a high price to earnings multiple has caused its share price to materially underperform the market. From the time I wrote the article until today, shares are up only 17.8% compared to the 29.6% rise that the S&P 500 (SP500) saw. Even so, I believe that the market continues to misjudge this opportunity. Admittedly, the low asset quality of the business is problematic. And because of that, I do think that having it rated a very soft "Buy" as opposed to a solid "Buy" is appropriate.

Upside remains

Fundamentally speaking, financial performance for Capitol Federal Financial has been pretty good as of late. As an example, we need only take the most recent quarter, which would be the second quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, net interest income for the business was $43.8 million. That translated to a rise compared to the $41 million reported the previous year. This came not only because of a rise in the size of the company's deposit base, and other changes to its balance sheet, but also because the firm benefited from an improvement in its net interest margin from 1.86% to 1.92%. For the first half of 2025 as a whole, net interest income of $85.4 million beat out the $80.5 million reported the previous year, with an improvement in the company's net interest margin from 1.76% to 1.89% helping significantly.

Outside of its core banking activities that bring in net interest income, the firm also has some other operations. Included under non-interest income would be deposit service fees that the business generates. The rise in the second quarter of 2025 from $2.5 million to $2.6 million, as well as the jump in insurance commissions from $0.7 million to $0.9 million, made possible an improvement in the company's non-interest income from $4.6 million to $5 million. And for the first half of the year in its entirety, the absence of the $13.3 million loss that the company saw from securities transactions resulted in its non-interest income going from negative $4.3 million to positive $9.6 million.

These improvements were instrumental in pushing up profitability for the company. For the second quarter of 2025, net income was $15.4 million. That compared favorably to the $13.8 million reported the previous year. Meanwhile, for the first half of the year, net profits jumped from $16.3 million to $30.8 million. Even though there were some one-time items in here such as the aforementioned loss on securities transactions, the overall trend is undeniably positive.

The company's balance sheet is also showing signs of improvement. For the most recent quarter, the value of deposits came in at $6.37 billion. This marks growth compared to the $6.13 billion that the company ended 2024 with. It's also up from the $6.19 billion reported for the end of 2023. One thing that I always look for is exposure to broker deposits. These types of deposits are not inherently bad, but they are more costly and not always reliable. These are essentially deposits that come from other financial institutions. As of the end of the most recent quarter, the firm had no broker deposits, and only 1% of total deposits were public unit certificates of deposit.

Even though there was growth recently when it came to the deposits on the company's books, the value of loans has been a bit lumpy. After skyrocketing from $7.20 billion in 2023 to $7.91 billion in 2024, there was a drop to $7.88 billion In the most recent quarter. Loan composition is certainly an important thing to take into consideration when making an investment decision in a bank. We do know that about 49% of the company's loans are one to four family mortgages that it originated. Another 28.3% of its loan portfolio consists of the same types of assets that were purchased from other parties. And the firm also had a tiny bit of exposure to one to four family mortgages that were dedicated to the construction of such properties.

By comparison, exposure to the commercial space is quite small. If we look at commercial real estate, commercial and industrial loans, and construction loans, we end up with only about 21.1% of the company's overall loan portfolio. And consumer loans account for the remaining 1.4%.

Recently, the value of securities on the company's books have been on the rise. After plummeting from $1.56 billion in 2023 to $856.3 million in 2024, there was an uptick to $961.4 million in the most recent quarter. Cash and cash equivalents have also been on the rise, with an improvement from $217.3 million last year to $340.4 million this year. And over the same window of time, the value of debt has declined from $2.18 billion to $2.14 billion. All things considered, these are some pretty positive moves if you ask me.

When it comes to valuation, the picture for Capitol Federal Financial is undeniably mixed. If we use adjusted net profits from last year that strip out one time events, we still end up with a price to earnings multiple of 17.5. That's well above the 6 to 10 range that I prefer to see in this industry. In the chart above, I compared Capitol Federal Financial to five similar companies. And what I found was that four of the five firms ended up being cheaper than it is. As part of my analysis, I decided to also look at both the price to book and price to tangible book multiples. These can be seen in the chart below. In both of those cases, the amazingly low reading of 0.71 that Capitol Federal Financial scores places that materially lower than any of its competitors that I compared it to. This disparity has played a big role in my decision to remain bullish about the business. The fact that only about 12% of its deposits are uninsured also contributed to this decision.

Besides the high price to earnings multiple, the only thing I really don't like is the fact that asset quality for the institution remains low. In the first chart below, you can see a rather disappointing return on assets reading of only 0.64%. This was lower than all but one of the five companies I compared it to. In the subsequent chart, I did the same type of analysis using return on equity. And once again, four of the five firms ended up being better off than Capitol Federal Financial is.

Takeaway

From the data at my disposal, I would make the case that Capitol Federal Financial, Inc. is far from being a fantastic prospect. But it's certainly not a bad one. The company tilts between being mediocre and being slightly positive, though I have opted to be more optimistic about it. This is based on its low price to book and price to tangible book multiples, the improvements that it has seen so far this year from a revenue and profit perspective, and some of the changes that we have seen with its balance sheet such as a reduction in debt, growing deposits, and growth for both securities and cash. At the end of the day, I believe that maintaining a soft "Buy" rating is the right decision.

All returns assume reinvestment of dividends and capital gains. Current performance may be lower or higher than that shown. It is not possible to invest directly in an unmanaged index. Share classes have different fees and other features. Institutional shares have limited availability and may be purchased at various minimums. See prospectus for details. Net Expenses Excluding Investment Related Expenses for Institutional shares: 0.85%; for Investor A shares:1.15%.

Commentary as of 03/31/25

  • The fund posted returns of 2.91% (Institutional shares) and 2.76% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the first quarter of 2025.
  • The fund's slight underperformance of its benchmark was driven by macro-thematic measures and positioning, while stock selection sentiment and fundamental insights provided support.
  • From a sector- and country-positioning perspective, the fund remained largely neutral. The fund had slight overweight allocations to the information technology and communication services sectors, and maintained slight underweight exposures to the utilities and consumer discretionary sectors. It had a slight overweight holding in Taiwan and maintained a slight underweight position in India.

Contributors

Fundamental and sentiment stock selection insights provided support during the quarter. Within fundamental insights, defensive quality measures favoring lower-volatility stocks helped to mitigate some losses as investors sought shelter amid market volatility. Fundamental valuation measures tracking valuations, earnings, and other financial statement data did well amid the market style preference. Insights designed to evaluate both retail and analyst sentiment using machine reading helped to correctly identify emerging market themes and drive a successful underweight exposure to the automobiles industry.

Detractors

The fund's modest underperformance was driven by select country and sector macro-thematic measures. Insights designed to track Indian mutual fund positioning struggled as macroeconomic headwinds continued to overwhelm fundamental drivers of stock prices. Positioning favoring countries that display relative fundamental and macroeconomic attractiveness also suffered, as geopolitics and trade policy took center stage. These motivated incorrect security selection in India and an underweight allocation to the country.

Portfolio management

Jeff Shen, PhD, Gerardo Rodriguez, Richard Mathieson, David Piazza

Top 10 holdings (%)

TSMC (TSM) 9.09
Tencent Holdings (OTCPK:TCEHY) 5.87
Alibaba Group Holding (BABA) 3.65

Ping An Insurance (Group) Co Of China Ltd (OTCPK:PNGAY) 1.49
Meituan (OTCPK:MPNGF) 1.41

Investment approach

Invests at least 80% of its assets in equity securities or other financial instruments that are components of, or have market capitalizations similar to, the securities included in the MSCI Emerging Markets (1) Index.

Further insight

Emerging market equities had a positive quarter, outperforming developed markets amid U.S. tariff policy uncertainty and doubts about valuations linked to artificial intelligence (AI). Latin America led emerging market performance, with Colombia driving gains and Brazil rebounding after a tough 2024. China was the best performer in Asia, led by Al progress and President Xi Jinping's meeting with technology firms. However, the country's performance moderated in March due to tariff uncertainty. Taiwan struggled due to semiconductor industry weakness and tariff concerns. Indian equities rebounded in March, driven by easing regulatory pressure on banks, rupee appreciation, and strong buying by foreign equity investors. In Europe, Poland's positive performance suggested optimism about a potential end to hostilities in Ukraine.

Important Risks: The fund is actively managed and its characteristics will vary. Stock values fluctuate in price so the value of your investment can go down depending on market conditions. International investing involves special risks including, but not limited to political risks, currency fluctuations, illiquidity and volatility. If the fund makes short sales in securities that increase in value, the fund will lose value. Any loss on short positions may or may not be offset by investing short-sale proceeds in other investments. The fund may use derivatives to hedge its investments or to seek to enhance returns. Derivatives entail risks relating to liquidity, leverage and credit that may reduce returns and increase volatility. Investments in emerging markets may be considered speculative and are more likely to experience hyperinflation and currency devaluations, which adversely affect returns. In addition, many emerging securities markets have lower trading volumes and less liquidity.

The opinions expressed are those of the fund's portfolio management team as of March 31, 2025, and may change as subsequent conditions vary. data.title: i3 Verticals Focuses On Public Sector, But Catalysts Are Unclear
data.description:
Investment Outlook

i3 Verticals (NASDAQ:IIIV) recently reported its FQ2 2025 financial results, beating earnings per share estimates but missing revenue expectations.

Management expects forward organic revenue growth to be in the single digits as the company has divested from healthcare and focuses its efforts on the public sector.

With little concrete information from the company on capital allocation, including stock buybacks, only moderate topline growth and an uncertain state and local public market spending environment, my initial outlook on IIIV is a neutral Hold.

i3’s Market And Approach

i3 operates in the public sector software market, with offerings aimed at the verticals of transportation, ERP, public safety, K-12 education, enterprise utilities and justice tech.

According to a 2024 market research report by Verified Market Research, the global government software market was an estimated $40 billion in 2023 and is expected to exceed $77 billion by 2031.

If achieved, this growth would represent a reasonably robust CAGR (Compound Annual Growth Rate) of 10.1% from 2024 to 2031.

Factors expected to drive this growth include a growing push for e-government service provision, digital transformation initiatives to improve citizen engagement and government information gathering, regulatory compliance, cloud computing advantages and the need to drive cost efficiencies.

Also, the chart below shows the expected growth trajectory of the public sector software market from 2024 to 2031:

The company operates in government-adjacent markets, which are significant in size.

i3 sells its software and services through its direct sales and marketing efforts as well as through independent software vendors, value-added resellers and other independent sales organizations.

In the most recent quarter, the company's revenue contribution percentage breakdown by type is 70% from software and 26% from payments, as the pie chart shows here:

I’ve prepared a thumbnail SWOT of the company's primary characteristics:

Strengths

  • Vertical submarkets focus, recent divestiture, expanding focus on higher margin offerings

Weaknesses

  • Smaller footprint after strategic sales of business units, seasonal fluctuations

Opportunities

  • Growing government spending in digital systems, integration of AI technologies to enhance offerings, strategic tuck-in acquisitions

Threats

  • Uncertain government spending environment, single sector focus, intense competition from larger players

Recent Financial Trends

Total revenue by quarter (columns) has grown sequentially due to SaaS growth and internal AI solutions creating new sales opportunities. Operating income by quarter (red line) has remained flat in the past two quarters.

Gross profit margin by quarter (blue line) has fluctuated seasonally as normal, with uneven improvement and no discernible trend. Selling and G&A expenses as a percentage of total revenue by quarter (orange line) have also varied seasonally with no obvious trend change. R&D expenses as a function of revenue (purple line) have remained essentially stable in recent quarters despite management’s continued investment in generative AI initiatives.

Earnings per share (Diluted) have generally remained within a range with the exception of a few outlier quarters from segment divestitures and other one-time actions.

(All data in the above charts is GAAP.)

For balance sheet results, IIIV ended the quarter with $7.7 million in cash and equivalents and $12.0 million in total debt, all of which was long term.

In the past 12 months, IIIV’s stock price has grown by 24% vs. that of the benchmark iShares Expanded Technology-Software ETF’s (IGV) gain of 29%, mirroring the benchmark more or less, as the chart indicates below.

Below is a handy major financial and operating metrics table for IIIV:

Metric

Amount

EV/Sales - FWD

3.5

EV/EBITDA - FWD

12.6

Rev. Growth Est. - FWD

-13.6%

FY 2025 EPS Estimate - FWD

$1.05

Price/Sales

2.5

Revenue Growth - TTM

5.6%

Net Income Margin

46.5%

EBITDA Margin

14.0%

Market Capitalization

$842,380,000

Enterprise Value

$759,190,000

Operating Cash Flow

$7,640,000

EPS (Fully Diluted)

$4.73

Free Cash Flow/Share

-$0.25

Seeking Alpha Quant Score

Hold: 2.70

(Source - Seeking Alpha)

The company's Rule of 40 performance is shown in the table below and has been lackluster:

Rule of 40 Performance (Unadjusted)

FQ2 2025

Revenue Growth %

5.6%

Operating Margin

6.8%

Total

12.4%

(Source - Seeking Alpha)

Capital Allocation

Over the trailing twelve months, free cash flow was $5.2 million, and capital expenditures were $2.4 million.

However, the company paid a hefty $21.8 million in stock-based compensation in the last four quarters, so free cash flow net of SBC was actually negative ($16.6 million).

The net dollar decrease in stock outstanding for the last four quarters was valued at $12 million.

SBC as a percentage of revenue over the trailing twelve-month period was 9.0%, a high figure for a mature company.

Net stock buybacks versus SBC have only been about half of the SBC it has issues, so there has been net dilution to shareholders.

While in FQ3 2024, the board announced a $50 million stock buyback authorization through August 8, 2025, only $11.5 million has been repurchased in reported quarters since then.

Management plans include the potential to use part of its $400 million credit revolver for further stock repurchases.

Why I’m Neutral On i3 Verticals

i3 has made several moves to strategically realign its business recently, with the sale of its Merchant Services segment to Payroc in September 2024 for $438 million in cash and its Healthcare Revenue Cycle Management segment to Infinx for $96 million in cash.

The firm also recently acquired a small utility billing software company to enhance its utilities offering.

The result of these divestitures and recent acquisition will be to concentrate the company's efforts on the public sector vertical going forward.

In the most recent quarter, i3 derived 79% of its revenue from recurring sources, down 1% from Q3 2024’s results.

i3's forward growth strategy consists of increasing its focus on the various submarkets within the public sector, more deeply integrating AI technologies into its product suite and making selective acquisitions.

Management believes that DOGE style cost-cutting may be starting to occur at certain state and local levels, which it views as a potential positive for its SaaS, user fee plus payment revenue model approach, but it is too early to tell.

Regarding valuation, the market is valuing IIIV at a forward EV/Sales multiple of around 3.5x on an estimated NTM organic revenue growth rate of 8% against a median Meritech Capital SaaS Index implied ARR growth rate of approximately 15% (Source).

Also, the benchmark index indicates a recent median Net Dollar Retention Rate of 110%. i3 has not disclosed its revenue retention rate performance.

The index of publicly held SaaS application software companies showed a forward EV/Revenue multiple median of around 5.0x on May 5, 2025, as the chart shows here:

So, by comparison, IIIV’s EV/Revenue multiple at 3.5x is currently a significant discount to the broader index average of 5.0x as of May 5, 2025.

i3 is facing headwinds from its Canadian business, and management has removed a $2.5 million revenue estimate from fiscal 2025 guidance.

Looking ahead, the company has improved its balance sheet with its recent divestitures and appears ready for potential future smaller tuck-in acquisitions as it focuses on various public sector submarkets at the state and local level.

But with topline organic revenue growth expected by management to be in the single-digits and no concrete plans for stock price support through buybacks, i3’s near-term future is a bit cloudy as public sector spending is under some degree of uncertainty that has yet to be quantified.

Given these uncertainties, my initial outlook on IIIV is a neutral Hold on lack of visible catalysts in the near term.

data.title: Upexi: The 'Strategy' Of Solana?
data.description:
By now, most in the financial world are likely aware of what Strategy (MSTR)(STRF) is doing with Bitcoin (BTC-USD). Through convertible notes, common stock issuance, and even preferred shares, Strategy is raising capital essentially any way it can in order to buy Bitcoin. What is less known, is that there are other companies attempting similar strategies with both Bitcoin and alternative digital assets. One such company is Upexi, Inc (NASDAQ:UPXI). Like Strategy, Upexi operates a legacy business outside of digital assets that is in clear decline. However unlike Strategy, Upexi is prioritizing Solana (SOL-USD) rather than BTC. In this article, we'll briefly look at Upexi's legacy business, examine the company's balance sheet strategy, and get into whether or not SOL is the right kind of asset for such a move.

Upexi's 'Re-commerce' Business & Bitcoin Mining

Prior to jumping headfirst in the world of crypto, Upexi primarily operated a 're-commerce' business where it sold wellness products direct-to-consumer and through Amazon's (AMZN) marketplace. Entities that were previously owned outright or partially by Upexi included Tytan Tiles, E-Core, VitaMedica, and Interactive Offers. Those assets have all been sold. The company still operates LuckyTail, PRAX, and Cure Mushrooms.

After significant revenue declines over the last several quarters and mounting net losses, the company found itself facing Nasdaq de-listing last November after failing to file both its fiscal 2024 10-K and subsequent 10-Q in a timely manner. Upexi regained compliance in late December. Prior to Nasdaq compliance issues, Upexi had already begun a restructuring that saw the company selling assets, brands, and eliminating debt. By the end of December 2024, the balance sheet had just $400k in cash/equivalents, $6.1 million in current assets, $4.2 million in net property, plant & equipment and $17.5 million in total assets. With $13.8 million in total liabilities - $10.6 million of which was current - and 1,045,429 shares outstanding at the end of the year, UPXI had a book value of about $3.60 per share.

This year, Upexi announced its shift in focus to Bitcoin and crypto through newly established and owned brands ChainBitMiner and QuantumHash. The former appears to simply be a holding company for Upexi's digital assets and the latter seems to be more mining-focused despite still being quite vague regarding the actual operations of the business:

The Company will look to capitalize with investments in Crypto mining with a main focus on Bitcoin mining. The Company does not initially plan to operate its own mining operations.

The quote above is from the Upexi's late-January press release. The quote below is current as of mid-May from the company's website:

Leveraging Hash-as-a-Service to mine our own coins, expand our digital portfolio, and create low-risk digital treasury levered growth. We seek to mine Bitcoin as well as high growth opportunity alt coins with immediate conversion to Bitcoin.

If I'm reading these accurately, it seems like Upexi's original plan was to scale BTC holdings through usage of cloud hash services similar to the ones offered by companies like Bitdeer (BTDR) and BitFuFu (FUFU). Which anyone who follows my work may know are often terrible deals for the mining plan buyers. In March, Upexi announced a letter of intent to buy a 2 MW Bitcoin mining facility but didn't provide much detail beyond the following:

The facility is currently utilizing less than half its available capacity output. Quantum Hash intends to update current equipment and add the latest and fastest mining machines available to maximize hash rate and maximize bitcoin mined monthly, upon closing.

As far as I can tell, active and prospective shareholders don't know where this mining facility is located or what kind of machines are currently hashing. More importantly, it appears as though the company has already pivoted priority again based on how it is allocating an enormous capital raise in April.

Solana Strategy

Less than two months after the company announced the Bitcoin mining LOI, Upexi raised over $100 million through private placement from several venture funds as well as prominent crypto advocates like Frank Chaparro, Arthur Hayes, Austin Federa, and Joey Krug. As part of this arrangement, the company issued 36 million common shares and has been deploying that capital into Solana purchases. After initially announcing the acquisition of 45,733 SOL for $6.7 million in late-April, Upexi subsequently added over a half million SOL in less than a month.

Announced Locked? Cost SOL Cost Basis
4/29/24 No $6,700,000 45,733 $147
5/6/24 No $23,216,705 155,767 $149
5/12/24 Yes $44,128,641 326,347 $135
N/A* No $10,154,654 68,867 $147
Total $84,200,000 596,714 $141

Source: Upexi, *Author's estimate based on Upexi cost basis and announced holdings

The table above shows announced and estimated purchases based on recent press releases. However, 596,714 SOL at a cost basis of $141 per coin is not an estimate; those figures were provided straight from Upexi in a May 12th press release. The company purchased over half its SOL stack at a discount because they're locked at a weighted maturity of 1.4 years. Meaning, after initially expressing interest in Bitcoin mining through both hash services and company-owned infrastructure, Upexi appears far more committed to the Solana ecosystem at this juncture.

So far, these acquisitions have actually worked out wonderfully. For $84.2 million, Upexi has acquired Solana tokens that have a current market value of over $104 million and the company is actively staking those assets for chain-native yield as well.

In Upexi's Spring investor deck, the company laid out its plan for accretive issuance through both common stock and convertible debt. The company specifically mentions in the deck how SOL ETFs and the return of memecoins are positive catalysts for SOL's price. I'm not generally a fan of memecoins and have expressed that in prior work. But if they are going to have a resurgence this year, Solana is likely to be the chain where it happens.

Is Solana The Right Play?

As of this article submission, Solana's reward rate from staking is 8.3%:

Keep in mind though that unlike Bitcoin, which is hard capped at 21 million coins, Solana doesn't have a limited supply. Instead, Solana has a 5.3% rate of inflation that declines over time. This gives Solana staking a 'real yield' of 3% before any appreciation or depreciation of the asset price. Looking at the 'real yield' on a quarterly average, we can see that the real yield line in purple has held up well in the 3-4% range despite nominal APY in general decline going back to Q4-22 - the declining inflation rate has supported real yield in spite of lower nominal returns.

Another consideration is fees from transactions. Like Bitcoin miners who make revenue from both issuance and transaction fee payments, Solana validators are paid through both token emissions and fees from network usage. The latter has been terrific for Solana since the start of 2024 as the network has grown share of fees among top smart contract blockchains:

Compared with some of the other top smart contract blockchains by market cap, Solana has generally fallen somewhere between 20-40% share of the market over the 8 months. Year to date, Solana has generated over $430 million in network fees which is second only to Tron (TRX-USD). And while one could certainly argue Solana's rally has been driven by the casino-culture that is memecoins rather than expansion of real utility through stablecoin usage, as long as memecoins have an incentive to exist, they will persist. Pump.fun - the interface used for memecoin generation - will be sharing 50% of Pumpswap revenue with coin creators in the future. This could perhaps drive more memecoins on Solana.

The Elephant In The Room

Keep in mind, UPXI did $4 million in revenue for the quarter ended December 2024 and generated a $1.3 million net loss in the same quarter. The underlying legacy business was worth about $3.60 per share at the end of December. With the $100 capital raise and nearly 40 million shares issued subsequent to the end of last year, we now have a company that likely has somewhere between $5 to 10 million in cash remaining after using $5.3 million of the $100 million capital raise for working capital and debt reduction as well as $84 million for SOL purchases.

So for a re-commerce business worth a few million at best, $10 million in cash at most, and $104 million in SOL that is earning a 3% real yield after inflation, investors can buy UPXI at a $373 million valuation - or nearly 4x the Solana held by the company. I doubt this needs to be said; but that's probably way too high a premium for exposure to staked SOL.

Closing Thoughts

Valuation aside, this is all quite the turnaround for an entity that was facing Nasdaq de-listing just a few months ago. Even the path to get here was a bit confusing. It looked like Upexi was making a play on raising capital to buy Bitcoin à la Strategy and Michael Saylor just a few weeks ago. However, it now appears as though Solana is the ecosystem Upexi is primarily banking on. Wise? Possibly. Provided the Solana can be staked for blockchain rewards, there's an argument to be made for acquiring SOL with the purpose of generating yield on those assets. Of course, things often come down to price. Even though Upexi may have purchased SOL at a great price, that doesn't mean $10 a share for UPXI is a good price. At a market cap just shy of $400 million and a little over $100 million in SOL - I'm going to go out on a limb here and call UPXI a 'sell.'

data.description: The following slide deck was published by Tower Semiconductor Ltd. in conjunction with their 2025 Q1 earnings call.
